docs.title: Generative AI + Prompt Engineering
docs.text: An 3-hour course focused on the practical use of Generative AI and Prompt Engineering.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Block I (30 min): Introduction to Generative AI
  • Block II (45 min): Tools and current landscape
  • Block III (60 min): Prompt Engineering (core)
  • Block IV (45 min): Practical applications and personalization (Gems/GPTs, etc.)

docs.title: Learning outcomes
docs.text:
By the end of the course, you will be able to:

  • Identify the capabilities and limitations of generative AI systems (text/image/video/code).
  • Choose the appropriate tool depending on the objective (productivity, search with sources, multimedia creation…).
  • Design structured prompts (role, context, task, constraints, format) and improve them iteratively.
  • Create a personalized assistant for a real use case (education, business, development, etc.).

Tip: use the menu on the left to navigate through the different blocks.

docs.title: Block I — Introduction to Generative AI
Objective
docs.text: Understand what generative AI is, what types of outputs it produces (text, image, video, code), and what its limitations are.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Foundational models and LLMs.
  • Context, tokens, and context window (why it “forgets” things).
  • Hallucinations: why they occur and how to mitigate them.
  • Best practices: verification, sources, and risk control.

Key concepts

docs.title: Mini-demo
docs.text:
1. Ask for a summary of a technical topic with a format (bullet points + conclusions).
  2. Ask for a rewrite adapted to a different audience (e.g., 1st-year vs 4th-year students).
  3. Observe how the results change when adding constraints.

docs.title: Quick activity
docs.text: Write a 3-line prompt to: - Explain a complex concept to a specific audience. - Request an output with a fixed format. - Include “if you don’t know, say so”.

docs.title: Quality checklist
docs.text:
• Is the task specific?
  • Are there clear constraints?
  • Is the output format defined?

docs.title: 1. Artificial Intelligence (AI)
docs.text:
Do you think ChatGPT thinks like a human?

Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to systems that perform tasks typically associated with human intelligence, such as understanding language, recognizing patterns, or making decisions.

AI systems do not think or understand in a human sense. Instead, they learn patterns from large amounts of data and use those patterns to produce outputs.

Think of AI as a student who has read millions of books and can answer questions based on that experience, but without true understanding.

Examples - Recommendation systems (e.g., Netflix, Spotify) - Automatic translation tools

Try it Identify AI systems you use in your daily life.

docs.title: 2. Machine Learning (ML)
docs.text:
How can a machine learn without being explicitly programmed?

Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI in which systems learn from data instead of following predefined rules.

Instead of writing explicit instructions, we provide examples and allow the system to infer patterns and make predictions.

This is similar to teaching a child what a dog is by showing many examples, rather than defining all its characteristics.

Examples - Spam detection - Image classification

Try it How would you teach a machine to distinguish between cats and dogs?

docs.title: 3. Deep Learning
docs.text:
What happens when problems become too complex for simple models?

Deep Learning is a specialized area of Machine Learning that uses neural networks with multiple layers to process complex data.

Each layer extracts increasingly abstract features, allowing the system to handle tasks that are difficult to solve with traditional approaches.

You can think of it as a sequence of filters, where each layer refines the information further.

Examples - Face recognition - Voice assistants (e.g., Siri, Alexa)

Think about it Why is recognizing a face more complex than detecting spam?

docs.title: 4. Generative AI
docs.text:
What if AI could not only analyze information, but also create it?

Generative AI refers to systems capable of producing new content such as text, images, or videos.

These systems are trained on large datasets and can generate outputs that resemble human-created content.

Think of it as an artist who has learned from thousands of works and can now create new ones.

Examples - ChatGPT (text generation) - DALL·E (image generation)

Try it Propose a creative prompt and see what kind of output an AI system can generate.

docs.title: 5. Prompt
docs.text:
How we ask matters.

A prompt is the instruction or input given to an AI system to guide its response.

The clarity and level of detail in a prompt strongly influence the quality of the output.

Compare the following: - “Explain AI” - “Explain AI to a 12-year-old student using simple examples”

Which one would produce a better answer?

Try it Rewrite a vague prompt to make it more specific and effective.

docs.title: 6. Prompt Engineering
docs.text:
What if asking the right question becomes a key skill?

Prompt Engineering is the practice of designing and refining prompts to obtain better results from AI systems.

It involves iterating on prompts by adding context, specifying formats, and introducing constraints.

A simple prompt can be progressively improved: 1. Basic request 2. Add context 3. Specify output format 4. Add constraints or conditions

Try it Take a simple prompt and improve it step by step to obtain a more precise and useful response.

docs.title: Block II — Tools and current landscape (45 min)
Objective
docs.text: Understand the ecosystem of tools (ChatGPT, DALL·E, Sora, Gemini, Perplexity, Meta AI, Copilot…) and when to use each of them.

docs.title: Quick tools overview
docs.text:
• Chatbots/Assistants: ideation, writing, analysis, and general support.
  • Image generation: sketches, illustrations, teaching materials, assets.
  • Video generation: prototypes, visual concepts, storytelling.
  • Search with sources: questions that require traceability/citations.
  • Code assistants: generation, refactoring, tests, explanation, and documentation.

docs.title: Practical comparison (guided exercise)
docs.text:
The same objective, three approaches:

  1. “Explain it to me” (conversational model)
  2. “Search with sources” (citation-based engine)
  3. “Do it in code” (programming assistant)

Tip: it’s not about “a better tool”, but about “a more appropriate tool” depending on the context.

docs.title: Activity
docs.text:
Choose a real case (teaching, business, development) and answer:

  • Which tool would you use?
  • What output do you expect?
  • What risks are involved (bias, confidentiality, accuracy)?

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Master techniques to control the level of detail, format, and quality of responses.

docs.title: Recommended structure of a “professional” prompt
docs.text:
1. Context
  2. Role
  3. Task
  4. Constraints
  5. Output format
  6. Example (optional but very powerful)

docs.title: Reusable template
docs.text:
Context:
Role:
Task:
Constraints:
Output format:
Quality criteria:

docs.title: Techniques we will practice
docs.text:
• Zero-shot vs few-shot.
  • Step-by-step prompts (plan → execution → verification).
  • Strong constraints (length, tone, tables, mandatory fields).
  • Modular prompts (copyable blocks).
  • Rapid iteration (refinement with A/B testing).

docs.title: Guided practice (step by step)
docs.text:
Case: generate an assessable activity (or a report) with controlled output.

  1. Base prompt (simple).
  2. Add role + context.
  3. Add constraints + format.
  4. Add quality criteria + verification.
  5. Save it as a reusable template.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Ambiguous objective.
  • Output format not specified.
  • Lack of data or context.
  • Not requesting verification / explicit assumptions.

docs.title: Block IV — Practical applications and personalization (45 min)
Objective
docs.text: Apply what has been learned to a real workflow and create a personalized assistant (Gems / GPTs / similar tools) for recurring tasks.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text:
• Generation of rubrics, assignments, and model solutions.
  • Feedback on submissions using fixed criteria.
  • Materials: summaries, examples, questionnaires.

docs.title: Business
docs.text:
• Reports, meeting notes, emails, proposals.
  • Document analysis and executive summaries.
  • Process support (checklists, SOPs, templates).

docs.title: Software development
docs.text:
• Refactoring, test generation, documentation.
  • Pull request reviews with quality criteria.
  • Migrations and explanation of legacy code.

docs.title: Personalization (demo + practice)
docs.text:
• Define the objective of the assistant.
  • Persistent instructions: tone, limits, format.
  • Prompt library: “shortcuts” for frequent tasks.
  • Evaluation: how to detect failures and improve.

docs.title: Final exercise
docs.text:
Create a “mini-assistant” for a real use case and prepare:

  • Master prompt (template).
  • 2 secondary prompts (variants).
  • Output verification checklist.

Expected outcome: something you can reuse tomorrow in your work.
